label: Editorial Serif
description: Reading-led blog variant with stronger typography and editorial hierarchy.
signatureMotif: editorial type hierarchy, generous reading rhythm, and calm framing
promptHints: Make reading comfort and article hierarchy more important than card-heavy grids.
Use typography and spacing to create authority before adding decorative layout moves.

layouts: Use a centered longform article column with a narrow 42–48rem measure, generous top padding, and quiet metadata above the title.
Build the homepage as a stacked editorial front page with one dominant featured story followed by clean text-led article rows.
Compose category pages as a two-column magazine index with article lists on the left and a slim contextual sidebar on the right.
Use article headers with oversized serif headlines, compact deck text, and a restrained byline block before the reading column begins.
Place newsletter or author bio sections as calm inset panels between content blocks, never as loud full-width promotional banners.
motifs: Pair large high-contrast serif headlines with neutral sans body copy, using spacing and scale shifts as the primary visual hierarchy.
Use warm off-white page surfaces, soft paper-like contrast, and thin neutral dividers to frame content without heavy card treatment.
Keep containers lightly rounded with subtle borders, but let typography and whitespace carry most of the visual identity.
Favor quiet editorial details like uppercase section labels, muted metadata lines, and generous paragraph spacing over decorative UI flourishes.
antiPatterns: Avoid dense card grids with image-first tiles, because they break the reading-led rhythm and make the blog feel like a media app.
Never use bold gradients, neon accents, or glossy CTA blocks that overpower the calm literary tone of the layout.
Avoid cramped multi-column body text or overly wide article measures that reduce longform readability and editorial authority.
Do not build the homepage like a SaaS landing page with feature sections, logo clouds, or raised marketing panels.
